retrieved_docs.title: Amyloid Beta Peptide -- Biochemical and Genetic Pathology
retrieved_docs.content: Among its functions, ApoE serves as a chaperone that facilitates amyloid plaque clearance. The ApoE4 isoform appears to be less effective in this role, thereby promoting amyloid-β deposition. [11] [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Lipoprotein A -- Testing Procedures
retrieved_docs.content: The precise measurement of Lp(a) in human plasma is significantly impacted by the structural variability of Lp(a) that results from apo(a) size heterogeneity. Different Lp(a) particles contain varying quantities of repeating antigenic determinants, and the responsiveness of the antibodies against these repeated epitopes can change depending on the size of the apo(a) particle. As a consequence, immunoassays using polyclonal or monoclonal antibodies specifically targeting kringle IV type 2 epitopes will tend to underestimate apo(a) concentration in samples with apo(a) of smaller size compared to the apo(a) present in the assay calibrator, and overestimate the apo(a) concentration in samples with apo(a) of larger size. [2] To address these challenges, researchers have developed isoform-independent assays that aim to accurately measure Lp(a) without the impact of apo(a) size polymorphism. For example, a monoclonal antibody (a-40) targeting a unique epitope located in KIV 9 of apo(a) has been used in the development of an ELISA that accurately measures Lp(a) without the impact of apo(a) size polymorphism. [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Amyloid Beta Peptide -- Biochemical and Genetic Pathology
retrieved_docs.content: Another well-established genetic risk factor supporting the amyloid cascade hypothesis is the apolipoprotein E (ApoE) polymorphism. The ApoE gene ( APOE ) encodes 3 major isoforms: ApoE2, ApoE3, and ApoE4. Among these isoforms, the ApoE4 allele ( APOE4 ) is associated with an increased risk of sporadic Alzheimer disease compared to the general population. This risk is higher in individuals who are homozygous for APOE4 than in those who are heterozygous, indicating a dose-dependent effect. Studies have demonstrated a strong correlation between APOE4 carrier status and amyloid plaque burden in both patients with Alzheimer disease and cognitively normal individuals.

retrieved_docs.title: Lomitapide -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Lomitapide could be beneficial for HoFH patients exhibiting insufficient response to PCSK9 inhibitor treatment, those with atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease and a baseline LDL-C of 190 mg/dL or higher, and those who demonstrate insufficient responses to statins, whether used alone or in combination with PCSK9 inhibitors or ezetimibe. Lomitapide rapidly binds to and inhibits microsomal triglyceride transfer protein (MTP) within the endoplasmic reticulum lumen, which impedes the synthesis of lipoproteins containing apo-B in both hepatocytes and enterocytes. This process reduces the production of very low-density lipoprotein and chylomicrons, thereby lowering plasma LDL-C levels. The safety and efficacy of lomitapide have not been established for heterozygous familial hypercholesterolemia. This activity elucidates the indications, mechanism of action, pharmacokinetics, adverse event profile, and other relevant interactions essential for interprofessional healthcare team members in treating patients with HoFH and related conditions.

retrieved_docs.content: The ApoE isoform ApoE2 is clinically significant due to its reduced affinity for LRP and LDLR. Poor binding of this isoform decreases hepatic clearance of IDL particles and elevates serum triglyceride levels. [6] ApoCIII is considered pathogenic, as it promotes atherosclerosis by delaying the clearance of ApoB-containing lipoproteins and prolonging their presence in plasma. [7]

Indication: reducing LDL-C in subjects with HeFH or ASCVD adjunctive to diet and statin therapy tolerated at a maximum dose. 284 mg (initial dose) 284 mg (at 3 months) 284 mg (every 6 months after that)

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Apolipoprotein B -- Fundamentals
retrieved_docs.content: ApoB-100 carrying particles such as LDL and lipoprotein (a) predispose to premature atherosclerotic disease (ASCVD).

retrieved_docs.title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Mipomersen is a 20-base pair, 2’-O-methoxyethyl modified antisense oligonucleotide complementary to human apolipoprotein B-100 messenger RNA. [3] Mipomersen aims to identify and hybridize with the human apolipoprotein B-100 mRNA via the traditional deoxyribonucleotide or ribonucleotide base pairing mechanism. The interface between the complementary mipomersen therapeutic oligonucleotide with the newly synthesized human apolipoprotein B-100 leads to the degradation of the messenger RNA. Most frequently, the hybridization of mipomersen to its target mRNA leads to the recruitment of RNase H1, a catalytic enzyme that degrades ribonucleic acid in the cellular cytoplasm. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Lipoprotein Lipase Deficiency -- Treatment / Management -- Novel Treatments
retrieved_docs.content: Recent human genetic research has identified several novel targets for lipid-lowering therapy, including apoC-III, angiopoietin-like proteins 3 and 4 (ANGPTL3 and ANGPTL4, respectively), apolipoprotein V, and ATP citrate lyase. These targets hold the potential for improving outcomes in LPL deficiency and related atherosclerosis. ANGPTL3 is particularly notable, as it inhibits LPL activity, and monoclonal antibodies have been developed to target this protein.

retrieved_docs.title: Mipomersen(Archived) -- Mechanism of Action -- Distribution
retrieved_docs.content: Studies in mice and monkey experiments have shown that mipomersen dose-dependently reduces apolipoprotein B-100 concentrations and has no effect on high-density lipoprotein cholesterol levels (HDL-C). Further experimentations have demonstrated that mipomersen achieves steady-state tissue concentrations following the 6-months mark post-administration. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Lomitapide -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Lomitapide rapidly binds to and inhibits microsomal triglyceride transfer protein (MTP) within the endoplasmic reticulum lumen, which impedes the synthesis of lipoproteins containing apo-B in both hepatocytes and enterocytes. This process reduces the production of very low-density lipoprotein (VLDL) and chylomicrons, thereby decreasing LDL-C levels in plasma. [11] Inhibition of VLDL synthesis decreases the secretion of apoB-100-containing lipoproteins. The reduction in lipoprotein(a) or Lp(a) levels is correlated with a significant decrease in major adverse cardiovascular events. Lomitapide administration has been associated with a significant reduction in lipoprotein(a) levels. [12]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Apolipoprotein B -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Familial defective apo B is an autosomal dominant disorder that arises due to a mutation in apo B that prevents binding of the defective apo B to the LDL receptor. This condition results in a clinical phenotype similar to classical familial hypercholesterolemia due to mutations in the LDL receptor with increased LDL-cholesterol, xanthomas, and premature ASCVD, underscoring the importance of apoB in atherogenesis. The most frequent variant is apoB-3500, caused by a point mutation resulting in a substitution of glutamine for arginine at position 3500. [1] This mutation is an excellent illustration of a defect in an important ligand resulting in premature ASCVD.

retrieved_docs.title: Amyloid Beta Peptide -- Clinical Significance -- Teaching Points
retrieved_docs.content: APP undergoes proteolytic cleavage to generate amyloid-β peptide. An imbalance in the production and clearance of this peptide appears to lead to oligomer accumulation and plaque formation. Amyloid plaques are the histopathological hallmark of Alzheimer disease. [19] [20]

retrieved_docs.title: Abetalipoproteinemia -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Explain how abetalipoproteinemia manifests in each of the three main organ systems that it affects. Describe anticipated laboratory findings of a patient with abetalipoproteinemia. Outline the treatment of abetalipoproteinemia. Review the importance of collaboration between interprofessional team members for improved recognition and treatment of abetalipoproteinemia. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 3 -- Community 1624
retrieved_docs.content: Atorvastatin, fluvastatin and pitavastatin are all members of the therapeutic class identified as HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. This class of agents works by inhibiting the HMG CoA reductase enzyme, which is the rate‑limiting step in hepatic cholesterol synthesis, thereby lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and reducing overall cardiovascular risk. Because they share the same mechanism of action, these statins are commonly used in the management of hyperlipidaemia and for primary or secondary prevention of atherosclerotic disease. Recognizing that atorvastatin, fluvastatin and pitavastatin belong to the same drug class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and anticipate class‑related effects and potential drug interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 110
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes a network of pharmacologic classes and their interrelations. β‑3‑adrenergic receptor antagonists include 2,3‑dihydrothiopyrano(2,3‑b)pyridine—chemically related to a pyridine scaffold—and L 750355. Lipid‑modifying therapy comprises plain agents such as HMG‑CoA reductase inhibitors (atorvastatin, simvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, pravastatin) and other groups: the antilipidemic benfluorex, nicotinic‑acid and its derivatives (which also exist as a peripheral‑vasodilator form), and bile‑acid sequestrants exemplified by colesevelam. All of these agents belong to the broader cardiovascular system drug category, which additionally contains peripheral vasodilators and enzymes that act as peripheral vasodilators. Simvastatin is formulated in a fixed‑dose combination with sitagliptin and is contraindicated with the HIV protease inhibitor nelfinavir (including nelfinavir mesylate). Colesevelam is contraindicated in patients with الْتِهابُ البَنكرِياس.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: drugs that do not contain insulin. Members of this class include EXENATIDE, LIRAGLUTIDE, and LIXISENATIDE. DPP‑4 INHIBITORS (DIPEPTIDYL PEPTIDASE‑4 INHIBITORS) are another subclass of non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents. Specific agents that belong to this class are ALOGLIPTIN, SITAGLIPTIN, and the fixed‑dose combination SITAGLIPTIN / SIMVASTATIN (the latter combines a DPP‑4 inhibitor with a lipid‑lowering drug). Alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors are also members of the non‑insulin glucose‑lowering drug group. BIGUANIDE BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS form a further subclass of non‑insulin agents. METFORMIN and the older agent PHENFORMIN (listed as “fenformin”) are members of the biguanide class. In summary, the therapeutic landscape for diabetes includes insulin‑based therapies (primarily long‑acting injectable insulins, some combined with GLP‑1 analogues) and a diverse array of non‑insulin glucose‑lowering drugs, encompassing GLP‑1 analogues, DPP‑4 inhibitors, alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors,

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
retrieved_docs.content: The community defines “DRUGS USED IN DIABETES” as comprising two major super‑classes: **INSULINS AND ANALOGUES** and **BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS, EXCL. INSULINS**. Within INSULINS AND ANALOGUES, the subclass **INSULINS AND ANALOGUES FOR INJECTION, LONG‑ACTING** includes agents such as **INSULIN GLARGINE**, the combination product **INSULIN DEGLUDEC/LIRAGLUTIDE** (which incorporates **LIRAGLUTIDE**), and **INSULIN GLARGINE/LIXISENATIDE** (which incorporates **LIXISENATIDE**). Non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents are organized into several pharmacologic classes. **ALPHA GLUCOSIDASE INHIBITORS** lower blood glucose by delaying carbohydrate absorption. **DIPEPTIDYL PEPTIDASE‑4 (DPP‑4) INHIBITORS**—including **ALOGLIPTIN** and **SITAGLIPTIN** (the latter also listed as a DPP‑4 inhibitor)—are classified under BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS, EXCL. INSULINS. **GLUCAGON‑LIKE PEPTIDE‑1 (GLP‑1) ANALOGUES** comprise **EXENATID**, **LIRAGLUTIDE**, and **LIXISENATIDE**. **BIGUANIDE BLOOD GLUCOSE LOWER DRUGS** represent another subclass; **FENFORMIN (metformin)** is a member, illustrating that biguanides are a subset of the broader non‑insulin glucose‑lowering category. Combination formulations link agents from different classes, exemplified by **SIMVASTATIN/SITAGLIPTIN**, which pairs the statin **SIMVASTATIN** with the DPP‑4 inhibitor **SITAGLIPTIN**. An important drug‑interaction contraindication is noted: **NELFINAVIR MESYLATE** is contraindicated when used with **SIMVASTATIN**. Overall, the taxonomy clarifies that insulin preparations (including long‑acting analogues) and various oral or injectable non‑insulin agents (alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors, GLP‑1 analogues, and biguanides) are organized under the overarching category of diabetes medications, with combination products and specific contraindications guiding safe and effective patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 3 -- Community 1557
retrieved_docs.content: S‑adenosylmethionin (S‑adenosylmethionine) and betain are both classified as members of the broader biochemical group “Amino acids and derivatives.” In addition, the therapeutic protein metreleptin is also considered a member of this “Amino acids and derivatives” category, reflecting its composition of amino‑acid residues. Recognizing that S‑adenosylmethionin, betain, and metreleptin share this common classification highlights their related metabolic origins and can inform their roles in physiological processes and potential therapeutic use.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 1225
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all classified as members of the therapeutic group “HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs.” This class of agents inhibits the enzyme HMG CoA reductase, thereby reducing hepatic cholesterol synthesis and lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels. As plain lipid‑modifying drugs, these statins are used to manage hyperlipidaemia and reduce cardiovascular risk in patients with elevated cholesterol. Recognizing that fluvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin share this mechanism of action and therapeutic category helps clinicians select appropriate statin therapy based on individual drug properties, efficacy, and safety profiles.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 1 -- Community 146
retrieved_docs.content: The community’s knowledge links chemical scaffolds, pharmacologic mechanisms, therapeutic indications, contraindications, and diagnostic uses across multiple drug classes. 2,3‑dihydrothiopyrano(2,3‑b)pyridine and the experimental agent L 750355 are classified as β3‑adrenergic receptor antagonists and share a pyridine‑derived core. The pyridiny heterocycle serves as a central precursor for cerivastatin, lafutidine, and is connected to zinc pyrithione; lafutidine additionally derives from a piperidiny scaffold. HMG‑CoA reductase inhibitors (plain lipid‑modifying drugs) comprise atorvastatin, simvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin and cerivastatin; simvastatin is formulated in the fixed‑dose combination simvastatin/sitagliptin and is contraindicated with the protease inhibitor nelfinavir (including its mesylate salt) because of a drug‑drug interaction. Rosuvastatin, the proton‑pump inhibitor rabeprazol and the anticoagulant reviparin are grouped under the formulation category “R

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 880
retrieved_docs.content: HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, classified as plain lipid‑modifying drugs, comprise a group of statin medications that share the same mechanism of action—blocking the HMG‑CoA reductase enzyme to reduce cholesterol synthesis. The specific agents that belong to this class are fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin. By lowering low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol and modestly raising high‑density lipoprotein (HDL) cholesterol, these statins are central to the management of hyperlipidaemia and the prevention of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Understanding that fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin, and pravastatin are all members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitor class helps clinicians select appropriate lipid‑lowering therapy and anticipate class‑wide effects and potential adverse reactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 3 -- Community 2063
retrieved_docs.content: Glucagon-like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues constitute a class of therapeutic agents that mimic the incretin hormone GLP‑1, enhancing glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, inhibiting glucagon release, slowing gastric emptying, and promoting satiety. Liraglutide is a member of this GLP‑1 analogue class, and its pharmacologic actions make it valuable for the treatment of type 2 diabetes mellitus and for weight management in patients with obesity.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 1 -- Community 233
retrieved_docs.content: 2,3‑Dihydrothiopyrano(2,3‑b)pyridine and the compound L 750355 are classified as β‑3‑adrenergic receptor antagonists and are chemically mapped to the pyridine family. The HMG‑CoA reductase inhibitor (plain lipid‑modifying drug) class includes atorvastatin, simvastatin, fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, cerivastatin and pitavastatin; cerivastatin and lafutidine are linked to the pyridiny scaffold, while lafutidine is also mapped from piperidiny. Simvastatin forms part of the fixed‑dose combination simvastatin/sitagliptin, a product that combines a statin with the DPP‑4 inhibitor sitagliptin; this combination is contraindicated when co‑administered with nelfinavir mesylate. DPP‑4 inhibitors for blood‑glucose lowering comprise alogliptin, sitagliptin and the sitagliptin component of the simvastatin/sitagliptin combination. H2‑receptor antagonists used to reduce gastric acid secretion include cimetidine (associated with guanidine chemistry), lafutidine, nizatidine, roxatidine and

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 2 -- Community 1191
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin, rosuvastatin, and cerivastatin are all members of the class of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs. As HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, these agents act by blocking the rate‑limiting enzyme in cholesterol biosynthesis, leading to reduced low‑density lipoprotein (LDL) cholesterol levels and a lower risk of atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease. Their classification as plain lipid‑modifying drugs underscores their primary therapeutic role in managing dyslipidaemia without additional pharmacologic actions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2120
retrieved_docs.content: Erythropoietin and glucagon are both classified as members of the broader category “Amino Acid, Peptide, or Protein,” indicating that each is a peptide‑based biomolecule. As a peptide hormone, glucagon can be managed therapeutically by an approach described as “رد فعل تأقي,” suggesting that this specific type of counter‑reaction or antagonistic intervention may be used in the treatment of conditions involving glucagon.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2743
retrieved_docs.content: Amfetamin is classified as a vasoconstrictor, and angiotensin amide is also identified as a vasoconstrictor. Both agents belong to the group of substances that cause narrowing of blood vessels, thereby influencing vascular tone and blood pressure regulation. Understanding that these drugs share vasoconstrictive properties is important for anticipating their hemodynamic effects and for managing patients who may be exposed to one or both agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 3 -- Community 1353
retrieved_docs.content: Fluvastatin and rosuvastatin are both classified as members of the HMG CoA reductase inhibitors, plain lipid‑modifying drugs, a therapeutic class used to lower cholesterol and manage dyslipidemia.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1541
retrieved_docs.content: L‑carnitine (L‑karnitin) and S‑adenosylmethionine (S‑adenosylmethionin) are both classified as members of the biochemical group “amino acids and derivatives,” reflecting their roles as small organic compounds derived from or related to amino acids that participate in various metabolic pathways. In contrast, metreleptin, a recombinant analog of the hormone leptin used in the treatment of certain metabolic disorders, does not belong to the amino acids and derivatives class. Recognizing that L‑carnitine and S‑adenosylmethionine are part of this broader amino‑acid‑derived category, while metreleptin is a distinct protein‑based therapeutic agent, helps clarify their differing biochemical natures and informs appropriate clinical and pharmacological considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 32 -- Level 1 -- Community 63
retrieved_docs.content: The therapeutic landscape for diabetes is organized into two principal families: insulins and analogues, and blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs that exclude insulins. Insulins and analogues comprise injectable formulations, including long‑acting insulins such as insulin glargine, the combination insulin degludec / liraglutide, and the combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide; the latter two products incorporate the GLP‑1 analogues liraglutide and lixisenatide, respectively. Blood‑glucose‑lowering drugs excl. insulins are divided into several mechanistic subclasses. Alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors lower glucose by delaying carbohydrate absorption. Dipeptidyl‑peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors, a non‑insulin subclass, include alogliptin, sitagliptin, and the fixed‑dose combination simvastatin / sitagliptin. Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues—exenatide, liraglutide, and lixisenatide—also reduce glucose without containing insulin. Biguanides, another subclass of non‑insulin antihyperglycemic agents, consist of metformin and phenformin (listed as “fenformin”). Together, insulin‑based regimens (both long‑acting and combination products) and the diverse non‑insulin agents—alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors, GLP‑1 analogues, and biguanides—provide complementary mechanisms to achieve optimal glycemic control in patients with diabetes.
